papers.title: Three New Late-type Hypervelocity Star Candidates from Gaia DR2 with Refined Selection Criteria
papers.author_names: Jiao Li Shi Jia Yan Gao Deng-Kai Jiang Thomas Kupfer Ulrich Heber Chao Liu Xue-Fei Chen Zhan-Wen Han

abstract.text: Several dozen hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates have been reported based on the second data release of Gaia (Gaia DR2). However, it has been proven that the radial velocities of some Gaia HVS candidates are not reliable. In this paper, we employ refined astrometric criteria to re-examine Gaia DR2, arriving at a more reliable sample of HVS and high velocity star candidates than those found by previous authors.We develop a method called Binary Escape Probability Analysis to identify some HVS candidates. This method allows us to work with stars having only two epochs of measured radial velocity. These stars were usually discarded in previous similar studies. A scrutiny of our final results sheds light on selection effects present in our studies, which we propose to be the focus of future studies. In total, we find three late-type (2 G-type and 1 K-type) HVS and 21 high velocity star candidates, 3 and 11 of which are new, respectively. Judging by their historical trajectories, which we calculate, all three HVS candidates could not have had Galactic center origins. Further monitoring is required to confirm their status.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper presents a refined method—Binary Escape Probability Analysis—to identify hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates using only two epochs of radial velocity data, overcoming limitations in prior studies that required precise radial velocity measurements. By reanalyzing Gaia DR2 data with improved astrometric criteria, the authors identify three late-type HVS candidates (two G-type, one K-type) and 21 high-velocity star candidates, three of which are newly discovered. Crucially, all three HVS candidates are ruled out as having Galactic center origins based on their historical trajectories, suggesting alternative ejection mechanisms such as disk-based supernova explosions or extragalactic origins like the LMC.

source_fields.title: Three New Late-type Hypervelocity Star Candidates from Gaia DR2 with Refined Selection Criteria

source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes a method that selects high-precision Gaia stellar data by applying stringent astrometric, photometric, and velocity criteria to derive reliable Galactic rest frame velocities and identify stars with velocities above the escape speed, based on a specific Galactic potential model. Summary: This study identifies three new late-type high-velocity star (HVS) candidates and 21 high-velocity star candidates, with radial velocities exceeding 500 km/s potentially unreliable due to low signal-to-noise ratios, and concludes that these objects are unlikely to have originated in the Galactic center. Discussion: The experiment identifies 16 hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates and 23 high-velocity candidates from Gaia DR2, finding that only one overlaps with Marchetti et al. (2018b)'s list due to a more accurate Galactic potential model, and concludes that 15 previously excluded candidates with few radial velocity measurements are likely true HVSs (unbound probability >92% via BEPA), while systemic parallax offsets and spectral contamination do not significantly alter the results. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to resolve the origin ambiguity of hyper-velocity stars by leveraging Gaia's high-precision proper motions to compute three-dimensional velocities and perform backward kinematic tracing, thereby addressing the limitation of relying solely on radial velocities and enabling more accurate determination of whether HVSs originate from the Galactic center, disk, or extragalactic sources. Binary Escape Probability Analysis: The BEPA method quantifies the binary escape probability by accounting for orbital velocity variations in radial velocity measurements, using a statistical criterion based on the ratio of observed median radial velocity to its velocity dispersion. Radial Velocities And Possible Contamination: Radial velocity measurements for high-velocity stars like HV22 and HV23 are likely contaminated by nearby brighter stars due to Gaia's slitless spectroscopy, leading to unreliable velocities and suggesting that only a subset of candidates have trustworthy radial velocities.

abstract: Several dozen hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates have been reported based on the second data release of Gaia (Gaia DR2).
This method allows us to work with stars having only two epochs of measured radial velocity. These stars were usually discarded in previous similar studies.
title: Three New Late-type Hypervelocity Star Candidates from Gaia DR2 with Refined Selection Criteria

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract explicitly state identification of 3 new HVS candidates and 21 high-velocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 using refined astrometric criteria. Paper presents a candidate list with object-level data.

papers.title: Runaway and walkaway stars from the ONC with Gaia DR2
papers.author_names: Christina Schoettler Jos de Bruijne Eero Vaher Richard J. Parker

abstract.text: Theory predicts that we should find fast, ejected (runaway) stars of all masses around dense, young star-forming regions. $N$-body simulations show that the number and distribution of these ejected stars could be used to constrain the initial spatial and kinematic substructure of the regions. We search for runaway and slower walkaway stars within 100 pc of the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC) using $Gaia$ DR2 astrometry and photometry. We compare our findings to predictions for the number and velocity distributions of runaway stars from simulations that we run for 4 Myr with initial conditions tailored to the ONC. In $Gaia$ DR2, we find 31 runaway and 54 walkaway candidates based on proper motion, but not all of these are viable candidates in three dimensions. About 40 per cent are missing radial velocities, but we can trace back 9 3D-runaways and 24 3D-walkaways to the ONC, all of which are low/intermediate-mass (<8 M$_{\\\\odot}$). Our simulations show that the number of runaways within 100 pc decreases the older a region is (as they quickly travel beyond this boundary), whereas the number of walkaways increases up to 3 Myr. We find fewer walkaways in $Gaia$ DR2 than the maximum suggested from our simulations, which may be due to observational incompleteness. However, the number of $Gaia$ DR2 runaways agrees with the number from our simulations during an age of $\\\\sim$1.3-2.4 Myr, allowing us to confirm existing age estimates for the ONC (and potentially other star-forming regions) using runaway stars.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The study identifies 31 runaway and 54 walkaway star candidates within 100 pc of the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC) using Gaia DR2 astrometry, with 9 three-dimensional runaways and 24 walkaways traced back to the ONC. Simulations of 4 Myr dynamical evolution show that runaway numbers decline with age while walkaway numbers peak around 3 Myr, suggesting observational incompleteness may explain the lower-than-predicted walkaway count. The agreement between Gaia-detected runaways and simulation predictions at ages 1.3–2.4 Myr confirms the ONC's age and demonstrates that low/intermediate-mass stars (≤8 M☉) are dynamically ejected in both runaway and walkaway modes, providing a new method for constraining star-forming region initial conditions via kinematic ejections.

source_fields.title: Runaway and walkaway stars from the ONC with Gaia DR2

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: Our simulations and Gaia DR2 analysis reveal that RW stars are rapidly ejected from the ONC, with only ~4–1 RWs remaining within 100 pc after 3–4 Myr, while all WW stars remain bound; among 9 confirmed 3D RWs (all low/intermediate-mass, 0.3–2.5 M☉), one potential RW (TYC 4774-868-1) and one binary (HD 36697) remain ambiguous due to RV discrepancies, and the observed RW count (7.3 ± 2.3 at 1 Myr, declining to 5.5 ± 2.5 at 2 Myr) aligns with simulations predicting a high fraction of low-mass RWs/WWs. Conclusions: This study uses Gaia DR2 data and $N$-body simulations to identify runaway and walkaway stars from the ONC, finding consistent age estimates of 1.3–2.4 Myr and demonstrating that ejected stars can constrain star-forming region initial conditions—though improved radial velocity and extinction data are needed for greater accuracy. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that links the dynamical evolution of young star clusters to the formation of both fast and slow runaway stars, addressing the problem of how low-velocity "walkaway" stars are produced in dense star-forming environments through dynamical ejections rather than binary supernovae. Results from Gaia DR2: The experiment identifies 31 runaway and 54 walkaway stars with isochronal ages younger than 4 Myr from 2D sky-position analysis, excluding 8 candidates with implausible ejection velocities or inward radial velocities, and finds that the brightest candidates—such as HD 288089 (K0, flight time > age) and HD 41288 (B5, ~2 Myr, ~44 km s⁻¹)—are consistent with recent ejection from the ONC, while υ Ori (O-star, ~24 M☉) is a rare walkaway reaching the main sequence. Gaia DR2 Data analysis: This section describes the methodology for analyzing Gaia DR2 data to identify runaway and walkaway stars in the ONC by correcting for perspective effects in proper motion, removing the Sun's peculiar motion, transforming data into a Cartesian rest frame centered on the ONC, and searching for 2D-candidates through backward integration of proper motions to determine minimum ejection times. Target and data selection: The Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC) is selected as the target region for searching runaway and walking-away (RW/WW) stars due to its proximity, youth, well-defined cluster boundary, and high stellar density, which enhance the likelihood of dynamical ejections, with velocity thresholds of 10 km s⁻¹ for WW candidates and 30 km s⁻¹ for RW candidates set to ensure true unbinding from the cluster. N-body simulations of the ONC: This section describes a set of 20 N-body simulations of the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC) with spatial and kinematic substructure, using a fractal dimension of 2.0, a subvirial initial state, and a Maschberger IMF with primordial binaries, to predict the number and velocities of runaway and walkaway stars for observational comparison. Appendix A Excluded candidates: This section lists runaway and walkaway star candidates excluded from the ONC sample due to radial velocities indicating either trajectories toward the ONC or required radial velocities exceeding |500| km s⁻¹. Appendix C Past visitors to the ONC: This section identifies past visitors to the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC) that were once part of the cluster's stellar population, with their 3D trajectories traced via runaway and walkaway stars, and their ages inferred from isochrone fits, indicating they are either main-sequence or pre-main-sequence stars older than the cluster's current age. Appendix B 2D-candidates with multiple, varying RV measurements: This section identifies 2D-candidates with inconsistent radial velocity measurements across sources that may indicate binary systems or warrant further investigation as potential 3D-runaway or walkaway stars.

abstract: Theory predicts that we should find fast, ejected (runaway) stars of all masses around dense, young star-forming regions.
$N$-body simulations show that the number and distribution of these ejected stars could be used to constrain the initial spatial and kinematic substructure of the regions.
We search for runaway and slower walkaway stars within 100 pc of the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC) using $Gaia$ DR2 astrometry and photometry.
We compare our findings to predictions for the number and velocity distributions of runaway stars from simulations that we run for 4 Myr with initial conditions tailored to the ONC.

title: Runaway and walkaway stars from the ONC with Gaia DR2

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper identifies 31 runaway and 54 walkaway candidates from Gaia DR2 within 100 pc of ONC. Provides specific counts of 3D-runaways and walkaways traced back to ONC with object-level astrometric data.

papers.title: The K supergiant runaway star HD 137071

abstract.text: Very few examples are known of red supergiant runaways, all of them descending from the more massive O-type precursors, but none from the lower mass B-type precursors, although runaway statistics among B-type stars suggest that K-type runaways must be relatively numerous. We study HD 137071, a star that has been considered so far as a normal K-type red giant. Its parallax measured by Gaia and the derived luminosity suggest that it is actually a supergiant, whereas its derived distance to the galactic plane and its spatial velocity of 54.1 km s$^{-1}$ with respect to the local standard of rest suggest that it is also a runaway star. However, intrinsic limitations in determining the trigonometric parallaxes of cool supergiants, even in the Gaia era, require accurate spectral classifications for confirmation. We reliably classify HD 137071 as a K4II star establishing its membership to the extreme Population I, which is in agreement with the luminosity derived using the Gaia DR2 parallax measurement. Kinematical data from the Gaia DR2 catalog confirm its high spatial velocity and its runaway nature. Combining the spectral classification with astrometric information, a state-of-the-art galactic potential model, and evolutionary models for high-mass stars we trace the motion of HD 137071 back to the proximities of the galactic plane and speculate on which of the two proposed mechanisms for the production of runaway stars may be responsible for the high velocity of HD 137071. The available data favor the formation of HD 137071 in a massive binary system where the more massive companion underwent a supernova explosion about 32 Myr ago.

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] The paper presents spectroscopic and astrometric evidence that HD 137071, previously classified as a normal K-type red giant, is actually a K4II red supergiant and a runaway star with a spatial velocity of 54.1 km s⁻¹. Using Gaia DR2 data and evolutionary models, the authors trace its origin to a massive binary system where a companion star underwent a supernova explosion ~32 Myr ago, supporting the dynamical ejection mechanism over the supernova disruption channel for K-type supergiant runaways.

source_fields.title: The K supergiant runaway star HD 137071

source_fields.sections_text: HD137071: HD 137071 is a high-latitude, bright K-type supergiant with peculiar motion and distance, suggesting it is a young runaway star rather than a normal Population I supergiant. Introduction: This paper presents the first compelling evidence for a nearby K-type runaway supergiant, HD 137071, addressing the long-standing observational gap in runaway stars among less luminous supergiants and advancing the understanding of dynamical ejection mechanisms in massive stars. Concluding remarks: HD 137071 is the first identified K-type supergiant runaway star, highlighting the potential abundance of such stars and the need for spectroscopic confirmation to accurately study runaway star ejection histories in the solar neighborhood. Results and discussion: HD 137071 has an effective temperature of 3900 K, a luminosity of $\\log L(L_{\\odot}) = 3.78 \\pm 0.13$, consistent with a luminosity class II, and shows no evidence of significant distance or extinction errors despite potential Gaia parallax systematics. New observations and spectral classification: HD 137071 is classified as a K4 supergiant based on spectral comparisons with MK standards, with its luminosity class determined through analysis of temperature-corrected absorption features such as the CaII triplet and other luminosity-sensitive lines.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Focuses on single star HD 137071 with detailed spectroscopic classification and Gaia DR2 astrometry to establish its nature as a runaway K supergiant. Presents object-level observational data.

papers.title: Deflection of the hypervelocity stars by the dance of the Milky Way and Large Magellanic Cloud
papers.author_names: Douglas Boubert Denis Erkal Alessia Gualandris

abstract.text: Stars slingshotted by the supermassive black hole at the Galactic centre will escape the Milky Way so quickly that their trajectories will be almost straight lines. Previous works have shown how these `hypervelocity stars' are subsequently deflected by the gravitational field of the Milky Way and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), but have neglected to account for the reflex motion of the Milky Way in response to the fly by of the LMC. A consequence of this motion is that the hypervelocity stars we see on the outskirts of the Milky Way today were ejected from where the Milky Way centre was hundreds of millions of years ago. This change in perspective causes large apparent deflections in the trajectories of the hypervelocity stars, which are of the same order as the deflections caused by the gravitational force of the Milky Way and LMC. We quantify these deflections by simulating the production of hypervelocity stars in an isolated Milky Way (with a spherical or flattened dark matter halo), in a fixed-in-place Milky Way with a passing LMC, and in a Milky Way which responds to the passage of the LMC. The proper motion precision necessary to measure these deflections will be possible with the combination of Gaia with the proposed GaiaNIR successor mission, and these measurements will unlock the hypervelocity stars as probes of the shape of the Milky Way, the mass of the LMC, and of the dance of these two galaxies.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper demonstrates that hypervelocity stars ejected from the Milky Way's center hundreds of millions of years ago are deflected not only by the gravitational fields of the Milky Way and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), but also by the reflex motion of the Milky Way itself in response to the LMC's fly-by. This reflex motion alters the apparent trajectory of the stars as observed today, introducing deflections comparable in magnitude to those from gravitational potentials—thereby making the observed paths sensitive to the true shape of the Milky Way's dark matter halo, the LMC's mass, and the orbital dynamics of the Milky Way–LMC interaction. Simulations of four scenarios—static Milky Way, flattened halo, fixed LMC fly-by, and dynamic Milky Way response—show that neglecting the reflex motion leads to significant errors in interpreting stellar kinematics.

source_fields.title: Deflection of the hypervelocity stars by the dance of the Milky Way and Large Magellanic Cloud

source_fields.sections_text: Results: Hypervelocity stars in the Milky Way–LMC system do not track back to the Galactic centre when the LMC's potential and Milky Way's motion are neglected, with up to 10% of stars deviating by more than 2 kpc, and future Gaia and GaiaNIR measurements can detect both the LMC-induced deflections and the Milky Way's 4.3 kpc, 29.6 km/s motion toward the LMC over the last 1 Gyr, revealing a significant bias in inferring the Milky Way's dark matter halo triaxiality. Conclusions: The motion of the Milky Way in response to the Large Magellanic Cloud induces significant deflections in hypervelocity stars' trajectories, offering a powerful new way to probe the Milky Way's dark matter halo and the dynamics of the Milky Way–LMC system, detectable with future high-precision astrometry from GaiaNIR. Methodology: The paper proposes an analytic method to simulate the production and orbital evolution of hypervelocity stars ejected by the Hills mechanism in the Galactic center, accounting for binary dynamics, main sequence lifetimes, and the reflex motion of the Milky Way and LMC to demonstrate the necessity of including these effects in interpreting observed hypervelocity stars. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of modeling the deflection of S5-HVS1's trajectory due to the non-spherical gravitational potential of the Milky Way and the dynamic interaction with the Large Magellanic Cloud, including the motion of the Milky Way itself, to constrain the dark matter halo shape and the galaxy's response to external gravitational encounters.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper about deflection of hypervelocity stars by Milky Way-LMC interaction. No observational stellar data or catalog presented; uses simulated trajectories.
